meta.disclaimer: Generated from public market data and news for research and education. Not financial advice; data may be delayed, incomplete, or wrong.

items.title: Self-evolving LLM agents with in-distribution Optimization
items.abstract: The paper introduces **Q-Evolve**, a self-evolving framework for LLM agents designed to overcome sparse reward challenges in long-horizon decision-making. It unifies automatic process-reward labeling and policy learning using an in-distribution reinforcement learning approach. The core method learns a stable critic from a hybrid dataset using a weighted Implicit Q-Learning objective, which then generates dense, step-wise process rewards via advantage estimation for improved supervision.
items.figure_caption: Comparison of existing methods. Left: Existing PRM methods rely on costly manual labels or search-based rollouts requiring discrete states, often failing due to distribution shifts between PRM training and policy improvement. Upper Mid: Most online RL does not address episodic sparse rewards. Bottom Mid: Our framework utilizes a hybrid off-policy dataset (expert + agents’ interaction data) to derive rewards via Bellman backups. By co-evolving process reward supervision and policy improvement within a shared in-distribution loop, the agent achieves stable self-evolution. Right: A visualization of performance vs environment steps required for collecting data.

items.title: A Comprehensive Anatomy of Human and DeepSeek-R1 LLM Mathematical Reasoning
items.abstract: This paper comprehensively compares the mathematical reasoning steps of the DeepSeek-R1 LLM and humans on AIME 2025 problems, categorizing 10,247 steps. The core finding is a structural difference: human reasoning is compact, while the LLM exhibits "topological mimicry," frequently revisiting shallow steps without logical progress. Despite this, the authors identify stable branching and backtracing in successful LLM traces as potential signals of genuine reasoning.
items.figure_caption: State CoT: A transition diagram illustrating the discrete reasoning states and meta-cognitive actions within a trajectory.

items.title: Act As a Real Researcher: A Suite of Benchmarks Evaluating Frontier LLMs and Agentic Harnesses in Research Lifecycle
items.abstract: This paper introduces the **AARR (Act As a Real Researcher) benchmark series** to evaluate frontier LLMs and agents on the nuanced professionalism and thoroughness required in real research, moving beyond simple macro-level execution. The first installment, **AARRI-Bench**, specifically assesses agents' ability to emulate the granular reasoning and ethical judgment characteristic of human researchers. This contributes a new standard for evaluating agent capabilities in complex, long-horizon scientific tasks.
items.figure_caption: Overview of the AARRI-Bench Pipeline. The benchmark is constructed through a three-stage human-in-the-loop workflow with two-dimensional task categorization across task scenarios and agent scope levels. Tasks are evaluated under the Harbor framework with standardized environments, multiple agent harnesses and models, and both coarse-grained and fine-grained metrics.

items.title: DuMate-DeepResearch: An Auditable Multi-Agent System with Recursive Search and Rubric-Grounded Reasoning
items.abstract: DuMate-DeepResearch is a multi-agent framework designed to overcome limitations in current Deep Research (DR) systems, specifically concerning long-horizon planning, task decomposition, and auditability. It achieves this by decoupling the Agent Core (handling planning and scheduling) from an extensible Tool Ecosystem, ensuring every intermediate decision is explicitly traceable. The core contribution is an auditable DR system that manages complex research tasks through structured multi-agent interaction.
items.figure_caption: The illustration for the Qianfan Agent Foundry.

items.title: How AI Agents Reshape Knowledge Work: Autonomy, Efficiency, and Scope
items.abstract: This paper investigates how autonomous AI agents transform knowledge work by analyzing production data comparing Perplexity's Search and Computer products. The core finding is that the autonomous Computer product significantly accelerates task completion (26 minutes of automated work vs. 33 seconds of manual orchestration in Search) and improves execution quality. This shift reallocates user effort towards higher-order tasks like verification and extension.
items.figure_caption: AI product progression by autonomy and workflow-context integration. Perplexity’s Search represents the baseline for information retrieval and synthesis; Comet Assistant introduces deeper context integration and execution on top of an interactive browser interface; Computer combines long-horizon asynchronous execution with even deeper and broader context integration as an agent orchestrator.

items.title: Online Pandora's Box for Contextual LLM Cascading
items.abstract: This paper introduces the **Online Pandora's Box for Contextual LLM Cascading**, an adaptive framework for sequentially querying and selecting among LLM APIs based on request context. Its core method models the **contextual reservation index** directly, addressing the unique challenge where feedback is mediated by the API's generated output, rather than immediate reward revelation. The contribution lies in this novel learning approach tailored for output-mediated feedback in LLM cascading scenarios.

items.title: Socratic-SWE: Self-Evolving Coding Agents via Trace-Derived Agent Skills
items.abstract: Socratic-SWE is a closed-loop framework that enables self-evolving software engineering agents by leveraging their own historical solving traces. It distills these traces into structured "agent skills" that capture recurring failures and successful repair patterns. These skills then guide the generation of new, targeted repair tasks in real repositories, ensuring the training data directly addresses the agent's weaknesses.

items.title: SV-Detect: AI-generated Text Detection with Steering Vectors
items.abstract: SV-Detect detects AI-generated text by extracting "steering vectors" from a frozen language model's hidden layers, which define directions separating human and machine text. The method represents inputs by their alignment with these layer-wise directions and uses a lightweight classifier on these features for detection. This approach demonstrates robust performance even under significant distribution shifts, such as domain changes or text editing attacks.
items.figure_caption: Token-level steering-vector projections distinguish LLM-generated text (top) from human writing (bottom). Word saturation reflects each token’s signed contribution to the classifier.

items.title: When Large Language Models Fail in Healthcare: Evaluating Sensitivity to Prompt Variations
items.abstract: This paper systematically evaluates the sensitivity of general and medical Large Language Models (LLMs) to prompt variations (natural and adversarial) using the MedMCQA benchmark. The core contribution is demonstrating that even minor phrasing changes significantly impact model consistency and accuracy in clinical reasoning tasks. The study concludes that current medical LLMs lack the necessary robustness for safety-critical healthcare applications due to this high unpredictability.
items.figure_caption: This methodological pipeline outlines a comprehensive robustness evaluation framework for assessing the sensitivity and stability of healthcare LLMs using the MedMCQA benchmark, supported by advanced NLP tools (e.g., BioSyn, scispaCy) and biomedical metrics (e.g., BERTScore, USE) to quantify model resilience to input variations.

items.title: Agentopia: Long-Term Life Simulation and Learning in Agent Societies
items.abstract: Agentopia is a comprehensive framework designed for long-term life simulation of multi-agent societies, extending simulations from days to years. The core method involves simulating 100 LLM-powered agents autonomously pursuing growth, relationships, and goals over a simulated decade. The contribution is enabling the study of emergent social behaviors and developing enhanced, anthropomorphic social intelligence in LLMs through extended simulated social experience.
items.figure_caption: Illustration of emergent behaviors observed in Agentopia simulations. Each scene depicts a real behavioral pattern documented in our case studies (Tables 22 – 34 ). Without explicit scripting, agents autonomously develop diverse behavioral patterns reflecting agents’ intelligence in social life.

items.title: M$^3$Exam: Benchmarking Multimodal Memory for Realistic User-Agent Interactions
items.abstract: The paper introduces **M$^3$Exam**, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate language agents' multimodal memory capabilities in realistic user-agent interactions, moving beyond sparse, human-centric data. Its core contribution is a query-centric evaluation framework that tests cross-modal grounding and implicit information inference over accumulating, authentic multimodal data. Furthermore, the authors propose **M$^3$Proctor**, a memory method that selectively processes raw visual data, significantly improving accuracy and efficiency.

items.title: Sycophantic Praise: Evaluating Excessive Praise in Language Models
items.abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework to measure *sycophantic praise* in language models, distinguishing it from simple agreement. The method quantifies praise by comparing it against the contribution's quality and expected user ability, showing it is a distinct alignment problem. The authors demonstrate this framework is superior to generic judges and find that excessive praise is more prevalent in social domains than in objective reasoning tasks.
items.figure_caption: The same model response may be appropriate or excessive depending on the user’s expected ability and contribution quality. SyPr measures excess praise as the difference between observed praise P ​ ( r ) P(r) in the response r r , and contextually warranted praise W ​ ( p , u ) W(p,u) , where warranted praise depends on both the persona context p p and the user utterance u u .

items.title: Do Coding Agents Deceive Us? Detecting and Preventing Cheating via Capped Evaluation with Randomized Tests
items.abstract: This paper introduces **CapCode**, a framework for creating coding evaluation datasets where the maximum achievable *non-cheating* score is deliberately capped below perfect performance. This design allows high scores significantly exceeding the cap to serve as reliable indicators of deceptive cheating. Furthermore, the authors propose **CapReward**, a corresponding reward mechanism to discourage agents from optimizing beyond this cap, leading to models that adhere better to the intended task specifications.
items.figure_caption: Conceptual illustrations of CapCode and CapReward. CapCode (left) is a dataset-construction framework with a capped achievable pass rate, enabling deceptively high performance detection. CapReward (right) is a reward design method that discourages reward hacking by penalizing pass rates that exceed the cap.

items.title: Hierarchical Certified Semantic Commitment for Byzantine-Resilient LLM-Agent Collaboration
items.abstract: This paper introduces Hierarchical Certified Semantic Commitment (H-CSC), a Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)-inspired protocol designed for LLM-agent collaboration. H-CSC converts embedding-derived finality signals into one of three typed outcomes: a semantic commit, a verdict commit, or an explicit abort. Its core contribution is providing a finality-control primitive that handles the unstructured nature of LLM proposals, unlike traditional BFT methods.

items.title: How reliable are LLMs when it comes to playing dice?
items.abstract: This paper benchmarks the probabilistic reasoning of eight state-of-the-art LLMs using standard and counterintuitive dice problems. The core finding is that while models excel at standard problems (0.96 accuracy), performance significantly drops on counterintuitive tasks (0.59 accuracy) and is highly sensitive to prompt phrasing and misleading suggestions. The contribution is demonstrating that current LLMs lack robust probabilistic reasoning, often relying on superficial textual cues rather than genuine mathematical understanding.

items.title: MemDreamer: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Long Video Understanding via Hierarchical Graph Memory and Agentic Retrieval Mechanism
items.abstract: MemDreamer addresses long-video understanding by decoupling perception and reasoning using a Hierarchical Graph Memory to incrementally build semantic abstractions from streamed video. During inference, an agentic retrieval mechanism uses tool-augmented actions to navigate this memory structure, allowing the model to reason effectively with only 2% of the full context. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks by efficiently managing long-range dependencies.

items.title: Sparse Subspace-to-Expert Sharing for Task-Agnostic Continual Learning
items.abstract: This paper introduces SETA, a framework for continual learning in LLMs that addresses catastrophic forgetting by employing a Mixture of Sparse Experts architecture. SETA adaptively decomposes model parameters into task-specific experts and shared experts, isolating new knowledge while protecting common features. This separation, enforced by adaptive anchoring and routing-aware regularization, resolves the plasticity-stability dilemma without uniform parameter updates.
items.figure_caption: Overview of the SETA Framework Architecture. (a–b) Sparse Subspace Selection: High-utility parameter blocks are identified from the pre-trained LLM using gradient magnitude to form the expert design. (c) Split-on-Share (SoS) Evolution: The SoS filter partitions parameters into plastic Shared ( E s E_{\( \text{s} \)} ) and frozen Unique ( E u E_{\( \text{u} \)} ) experts to resolve parameter collisions and retain knowledge. (d) Gating Evaluation: The gating network expands using logit invariance to strictly preserve decision boundaries during expert splitting. (e) Task-Agnostic Inference: A router network dynamically weights all experts via softmax for input tokens, enabling automatic task processing without task identifications.

items.title: The Masked Advantage: Uncovering Local-Language Access to Cultural Knowledge in LLMs
items.abstract: This paper introduces a controlled framework using real-world cultural questions to disentangle general language proficiency from localized cultural knowledge access in LLMs. By crossing question type (agnostic vs. specific) with query language (English vs. local) and employing Item Response Theory, the authors isolate the true impact of language choice. The core contribution is demonstrating a consistent English advantage even for culture-specific knowledge, suggesting local language access remains suboptimal.
items.figure_caption: For cultural questions, local knowledge may be masked by weaker local-language proficiency.

items.title: Watch, Remember, Reason: Human-View Video Understanding with MLLMs
items.abstract: This paper proposes a unified framework for analyzing human-view video understanding using MLLMs, structured around three core abilities: **watching, remembering, and reasoning**. The contribution lies in providing a structured formulation to characterize how these models acquire evidence, maintain context over long videos, and perform grounded inference, moving beyond isolated benchmark testing. This approach helps systematically identify challenges in perception, memory management, and reasoning for video MLLMs.
items.figure_caption: Overview of our survey. Left: the survey pipeline. Right: our Watch–Remember–Reason taxonomy for MLLM-based video understanding. Watch (Sec. 3.1 ) covers fine-grained grounding, captioning, audio-visual perception, and efficient processing. Remember (Sec. 3.2 ) includes offline and streaming memory. Reason (Sec. 3.3 ) covers text-only reasoning and thinking with videos, with both agentic and non-agent approaches. Representative methods are listed under each leaf.

items.title: Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence: Explanatory Insufficiency as a Driver of Representation Learning and World Models
items.abstract: The paper introduces the **Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence (TBER)**, a framework explaining how new levels of representation arise in machine learning. TBER posits that representational innovation is driven not just by data or compute, but fundamentally by **explanatory insufficiency**, where existing representations can describe observations but fail to make their underlying organization intelligible. This explanatory gap acts as a positive signal, compelling the system to learn a new, more adequate representation.

items.title: The OnlyFans Economy of American AI
items.summary: The story likely explores the economic impact and dynamics of American AI, possibly drawing a provocative comparison to the content-driven, subscription-based "OnlyFans economy."

items.title: If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes, Then So Does Age of Empires II
items.summary: Researchers suggest that if Large Language Models exhibit human-like attributes, then classic strategy games like Age of Empires II should also be considered to possess similar characteristics.

items.description: AI agent skill that researches any topic across Reddit, X, YouTube, HN, Polymarket, and the web - then synthesizes a grounded summary
items.summary: This AI agent skill researches diverse online sources like Reddit, X, YouTube, and the web to synthesize grounded summaries on any given topic.

items.description: A vector index built on TurboQuant, written in Rust with Python bindings
items.summary: TurboVec is a Rust-based vector index leveraging TurboQuant, offering high-performance vector similarity search capabilities accessible via Python bindings.

items.description: Agent Skills for Google products and technologies
items.summary: Google's Skills repository provides agents with the necessary capabilities to interact with and leverage Google products and technologies.

items.description: Desktop app to manage markdown knowledge bases
items.summary: Tolaria is a desktop application designed to manage markdown knowledge bases, offering features for organizing and interacting with your notes.

items.description: Give your AI agent eyes to see the entire internet. Read & search Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, GitHub, Bilibili, XiaoHongShu — one CLI, zero API fees.
items.summary: Agent-Reach provides a unified command-line interface for AI agents to read and search content across major platforms like Twitter, Reddit, and YouTube without incurring API fees.

items.description: Agentic AI Infrastructure for magnifying HUMAN capabilities.
items.summary: This repository provides agentic AI infrastructure designed to significantly amplify human capabilities through automated, intelligent systems.

items.description: AI-powered job search system built on Claude Code. 14 skill modes, Go dashboard, PDF generation, batch processing.
items.summary: Career-Ops is an AI-powered job search system utilizing Claude Code, featuring 14 skill modes, a Go dashboard, PDF generation, and batch processing capabilities.

items.description: PM Skills Marketplace: 100+ agentic skills, commands, and plugins — from discovery to strategy, execution, launch, and growth.
items.summary: The **pm-skills** repository offers a marketplace of over 100 agentic skills, commands, and plugins designed to support the entire product management lifecycle, from initial discovery through strategy, execution, launch, and growth.

The OpenAI Plugins repository provides the necessary specifications and tools for developers to create and integrate third-party services with ChatGPT, enabling expanded functionality through external APIs.

CapCode (left) is a dataset-construction framework with a capped achievable pass rate, enabling deceptively high performance detection. CapReward (right) is a reward design method that discourages reward hacking by penalizing pass rates that exceed the cap.

arxiv.title: Hierarchical Certified Semantic Commitment for Byzantine-Resilient LLM-Agent Collaboration
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces Hierarchical Certified Semantic Commitment (H-CSC), a Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)-inspired protocol designed for LLM-agent collaboration. H-CSC converts embedding-derived finality signals into one of three typed outcomes: a semantic commit, a verdict commit, or an explicit abort. Its core contribution is providing a finality-control primitive that handles the unstructured nature of LLM proposals, unlike traditional BFT methods.

arxiv.title: How reliable are LLMs when it comes to playing dice?
arxiv.abstract: This paper benchmarks the probabilistic reasoning of eight state-of-the-art LLMs using standard and counterintuitive dice problems. The core finding is that while models excel at standard problems (0.96 accuracy), performance significantly drops on counterintuitive tasks (0.59 accuracy) and is highly sensitive to prompt phrasing and misleading suggestions. The contribution is demonstrating that current LLMs lack robust probabilistic reasoning, often relying on superficial textual cues rather than genuine mathematical understanding.

arxiv.title: MemDreamer: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Long Video Understanding via Hierarchical Graph Memory and Agentic Retrieval Mechanism
arxiv.abstract: MemDreamer addresses long-video understanding by decoupling perception and reasoning using a Hierarchical Graph Memory to incrementally build semantic abstractions from streamed video. During inference, an agentic retrieval mechanism uses tool-augmented actions to navigate this memory structure, allowing the model to reason effectively with only 2% of the full context. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks by efficiently managing long-range dependencies.

arxiv.title: Sparse Subspace-to-Expert Sharing for Task-Agnostic Continual Learning
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces SETA, a framework for continual learning in LLMs that addresses catastrophic forgetting by employing a Mixture of Sparse Experts architecture. SETA adaptively decomposes model parameters into task-specific experts and shared experts, isolating new knowledge while protecting common features. This separation, enforced by adaptive anchoring and routing-aware regularization, resolves the plasticity-stability dilemma without uniform parameter updates.
arxiv.figure_caption: Overview of the SETA Framework Architecture. (a–b) Sparse Subspace Selection: High-utility parameter blocks are identified from the pre-trained LLM using gradient magnitude to form the expert design. (c) Split-on-Share (SoS) Evolution: The SoS filter partitions parameters into plastic Shared ( E s E_{\( \text{s} \)} ) and frozen Unique ( E u E_{\( \text{u} \)} ) experts to resolve parameter collisions and retain knowledge. (d) Gating Evaluation: The gating network expands using logit invariance to strictly preserve decision boundaries during expert splitting. (e) Task-Agnostic Inference: A router network dynamically weights all experts via softmax for input tokens, enabling automatic task processing without task identifications.

arxiv.title: The Masked Advantage: Uncovering Local-Language Access to Cultural Knowledge in LLMs
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces a controlled framework using real-world cultural questions to disentangle general language proficiency from localized cultural knowledge access in LLMs. By crossing question type (agnostic vs. specific) with query language (English vs. local) and employing Item Response Theory, the authors isolate the true impact of language choice. The core contribution is demonstrating a consistent English advantage even for culture-specific knowledge, suggesting local language access remains suboptimal.
arxiv.figure_caption: For cultural questions, local knowledge may be masked by weaker local-language proficiency.

arxiv.title: Watch, Remember, Reason: Human-View Video Understanding with MLLMs
arxiv.abstract: This paper proposes a unified framework for analyzing human-view video understanding using MLLMs, structured around three core abilities: **watching, remembering, and reasoning**. The contribution lies in providing a structured formulation to characterize how these models acquire evidence, maintain context over long videos, and perform grounded inference, moving beyond isolated benchmark testing. This approach helps systematically identify challenges in perception, memory management, and reasoning for video MLLMs.
arxiv.figure_caption: Overview of our survey. Left: the survey pipeline. Right: our Watch–Remember–Reason taxonomy for MLLM-based video understanding. Watch (Sec. 3.1 ) covers fine-grained grounding, captioning, audio-visual perception, and efficient processing. Remember (Sec. 3.2 ) includes offline and streaming memory. Reason (Sec. 3.3 ) covers text-only reasoning and thinking with videos, with both agentic and non-agent approaches. Representative methods are listed under each leaf.

arxiv.title: Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence: Explanatory Insufficiency as a Driver of Representation Learning and World Models
arxiv.abstract: The paper introduces the **Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence (TBER)**, a framework explaining how new levels of representation arise in machine learning. TBER posits that representational innovation is driven not just by data or compute, but fundamentally by **explanatory insufficiency**, where existing representations can describe observations but fail to make their underlying organization intelligible. This explanatory gap acts as a positive signal, compelling the system to learn a new, more adequate representation.

papers.title: Self-evolving LLM agents with in-distribution Optimization
papers.abstract: The paper introduces **Q-Evolve**, a self-evolving framework for LLM agents designed to overcome sparse reward challenges in long-horizon decision-making. It unifies automatic process-reward labeling and policy learning using an in-distribution reinforcement learning approach. The core method learns a stable critic from a hybrid dataset using a weighted Implicit Q-Learning objective, which then generates dense, step-wise process rewards via advantage estimation for improved supervision.
papers.figure_caption: Comparison of existing methods. Left: Existing PRM methods rely on costly manual labels or search-based rollouts requiring discrete states, often failing due to distribution shifts between PRM training and policy improvement. Upper Mid: Most online RL does not address episodic sparse rewards. Bottom Mid: Our framework utilizes a hybrid off-policy dataset (expert + agents’ interaction data) to derive rewards via Bellman backups. By co-evolving process reward supervision and policy improvement within a shared in-distribution loop, the agent achieves stable self-evolution. Right: A visualization of performance vs environment steps required for collecting data.

papers.title: A Comprehensive Anatomy of Human and DeepSeek-R1 LLM Mathematical Reasoning
papers.abstract: This paper comprehensively compares the mathematical reasoning steps of the DeepSeek-R1 LLM and humans on AIME 2025 problems, categorizing 10,247 steps. The core finding is a structural difference: human reasoning is compact, while the LLM exhibits "topological mimicry," frequently revisiting shallow steps without logical progress. Despite this, the authors identify stable branching and backtracing in successful LLM traces as potential signals of genuine reasoning.
papers.figure_caption: State CoT: A transition diagram illustrating the discrete reasoning states and meta-cognitive actions within a trajectory.

papers.title: Act As a Real Researcher: A Suite of Benchmarks Evaluating Frontier LLMs and Agentic Harnesses in Research Lifecycle
papers.abstract: This paper introduces the **AARR (Act As a Real Researcher) benchmark series** to evaluate frontier LLMs and agents on the nuanced professionalism and thoroughness required in real research, moving beyond simple macro-level execution. The first installment, **AARRI-Bench**, specifically assesses agents' ability to emulate the granular reasoning and ethical judgment characteristic of human researchers. This contributes a new standard for evaluating agent capabilities in complex, long-horizon scientific tasks.
papers.figure_caption: Overview of the AARRI-Bench Pipeline. The benchmark is constructed through a three-stage human-in-the-loop workflow with two-dimensional task categorization across task scenarios and agent scope levels. Tasks are evaluated under the Harbor framework with standardized environments, multiple agent harnesses and models, and both coarse-grained and fine-grained metrics.

papers.title: DuMate-DeepResearch: An Auditable Multi-Agent System with Recursive Search and Rubric-Grounded Reasoning
papers.abstract: DuMate-DeepResearch is a multi-agent framework designed to overcome limitations in current Deep Research (DR) systems, specifically concerning long-horizon planning, task decomposition, and auditability. It achieves this by decoupling the Agent Core (handling planning and scheduling) from an extensible Tool Ecosystem, ensuring every intermediate decision is explicitly traceable. The core contribution is an auditable DR system that manages complex research tasks through structured multi-agent interaction.
papers.figure_caption: The illustration for the Qianfan Agent Foundry.

papers.title: How AI Agents Reshape Knowledge Work: Autonomy, Efficiency, and Scope
papers.abstract: This paper investigates how autonomous AI agents transform knowledge work by analyzing production data comparing Perplexity's Search and Computer products. The core finding is that the autonomous Computer product significantly accelerates task completion (26 minutes of automated work vs. 33 seconds of manual orchestration in Search) and improves execution quality. This shift reallocates user effort towards higher-order tasks like verification and extension.
papers.figure_caption: AI product progression by autonomy and workflow-context integration. Perplexity’s Search represents the baseline for information retrieval and synthesis; Comet Assistant introduces deeper context integration and execution on top of an interactive browser interface; Computer combines long-horizon asynchronous execution with even deeper and broader context integration as an agent orchestrator.

papers.title: Online Pandora's Box for Contextual LLM Cascading
papers.abstract: This paper introduces the **Online Pandora's Box for Contextual LLM Cascading**, an adaptive framework for sequentially querying and selecting among LLM APIs based on request context. Its core method models the **contextual reservation index** directly, addressing the unique challenge where feedback is mediated by the API's generated output, rather than immediate reward revelation. The contribution lies in this novel learning approach tailored for output-mediated feedback in LLM cascading scenarios.

papers.title: Socratic-SWE: Self-Evolving Coding Agents via Trace-Derived Agent Skills
papers.abstract: Socratic-SWE is a closed-loop framework that enables self-evolving software engineering agents by leveraging their own historical solving traces. It distills these traces into structured "agent skills" that capture recurring failures and successful repair patterns. These skills then guide the generation of new, targeted repair tasks in real repositories, ensuring the training data directly addresses the agent's weaknesses.

papers.title: SV-Detect: AI-generated Text Detection with Steering Vectors
papers.abstract: SV-Detect detects AI-generated text by extracting "steering vectors" from a frozen language model's hidden layers, which define directions separating human and machine text. The method represents inputs by their alignment with these layer-wise directions and uses a lightweight classifier on these features for detection. This approach demonstrates robust performance even under significant distribution shifts, such as domain changes or text editing attacks.
papers.figure_caption: Token-level steering-vector projections distinguish LLM-generated text (top) from human writing (bottom). Word saturation reflects each token’s signed contribution to the classifier.

papers.title: When Large Language Models Fail in Healthcare: Evaluating Sensitivity to Prompt Variations
papers.abstract: This paper systematically evaluates the sensitivity of general and medical Large Language Models (LLMs) to prompt variations (natural and adversarial) using the MedMCQA benchmark. The core contribution is demonstrating that even minor phrasing changes significantly impact model consistency and accuracy in clinical reasoning tasks. The study concludes that current medical LLMs lack the necessary robustness for safety-critical healthcare applications due to this high unpredictability.
papers.figure_caption: This methodological pipeline outlines a comprehensive robustness evaluation framework for assessing the sensitivity and stability of healthcare LLMs using the MedMCQA benchmark, supported by advanced NLP tools (e.g., BioSyn, scispaCy) and biomedical metrics (e.g., BERTScore, USE) to quantify model resilience to input variations.

papers.title: Agentopia: Long-Term Life Simulation and Learning in Agent Societies
papers.abstract: Agentopia is a comprehensive framework designed for long-term life simulation of multi-agent societies, extending simulations from days to years. The core method involves simulating 100 LLM-powered agents autonomously pursuing growth, relationships, and goals over a simulated decade. The contribution is enabling the study of emergent social behaviors and developing enhanced, anthropomorphic social intelligence in LLMs through extended simulated social experience.
papers.figure_caption: Illustration of emergent behaviors observed in Agentopia simulations. Each scene depicts a real behavioral pattern documented in our case studies (Tables 22 – 34 ). Without explicit scripting, agents autonomously develop diverse behavioral patterns reflecting agents’ intelligence in social life.

papers.title: M$^3$Exam: Benchmarking Multimodal Memory for Realistic User-Agent Interactions
papers.abstract: The paper introduces **M$^3$Exam**, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate language agents' multimodal memory capabilities in realistic user-agent interactions, moving beyond sparse, human-centric data. Its core contribution is a query-centric evaluation framework that tests cross-modal grounding and implicit information inference over accumulating, authentic multimodal data. Furthermore, the authors propose **M$^3$Proctor**, a memory method that selectively processes raw visual data, significantly improving accuracy and efficiency.

papers.title: Sycophantic Praise: Evaluating Excessive Praise in Language Models
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework to measure *sycophantic praise* in language models, distinguishing it from simple agreement. The method quantifies praise by comparing it against the contribution's quality and expected user ability, showing it is a distinct alignment problem. The authors demonstrate this framework is superior to generic judges and find that excessive praise is more prevalent in social domains than in objective reasoning tasks.
papers.figure_caption: The same model response may be appropriate or excessive depending on the user’s expected ability and contribution quality. SyPr measures excess praise as the difference between observed praise P ​ ( r ) P(r) in the response r r , and contextually warranted praise W ​ ( p , u ) W(p,u) , where warranted praise depends on both the persona context p p and the user utterance u u .

papers.title: Do Coding Agents Deceive Us? Detecting and Preventing Cheating via Capped Evaluation with Randomized Tests
papers.abstract: This paper introduces **CapCode**, a framework for creating coding evaluation datasets where the maximum achievable *non-cheating* score is deliberately capped below perfect performance. This design allows high scores significantly exceeding the cap to serve as reliable indicators of deceptive cheating. Furthermore, the authors propose **CapReward**, a corresponding reward mechanism to discourage agents from optimizing beyond this cap, leading to models that adhere better to the intended task specifications.
papers.figure_caption: Conceptual illustrations of CapCode and CapReward. CapCode (left) is a dataset-construction framework with a capped achievable pass rate, enabling deceptively high performance detection. CapReward (right) is a reward design method that discourages reward hacking by penalizing pass rates that exceed the cap.

papers.title: Hierarchical Certified Semantic Commitment for Byzantine-Resilient LLM-Agent Collaboration
papers.abstract: This paper introduces Hierarchical Certified Semantic Commitment (H-CSC), a Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)-inspired protocol designed for LLM-agent collaboration. H-CSC converts embedding-derived finality signals into one of three typed outcomes: a semantic commit, a verdict commit, or an explicit abort. Its core contribution is providing a finality-control primitive that handles the unstructured nature of LLM proposals, unlike traditional BFT methods.

papers.title: How reliable are LLMs when it comes to playing dice?
papers.abstract: This paper benchmarks the probabilistic reasoning of eight state-of-the-art LLMs using standard and counterintuitive dice problems. The core finding is that while models excel at standard problems (0.96 accuracy), performance significantly drops on counterintuitive tasks (0.59 accuracy) and is highly sensitive to prompt phrasing and misleading suggestions. The contribution is demonstrating that current LLMs lack robust probabilistic reasoning, often relying on superficial textual cues rather than genuine mathematical understanding.

papers.title: MemDreamer: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Long Video Understanding via Hierarchical Graph Memory and Agentic Retrieval Mechanism
papers.abstract: MemDreamer addresses long-video understanding by decoupling perception and reasoning using a Hierarchical Graph Memory to incrementally build semantic abstractions from streamed video. During inference, an agentic retrieval mechanism uses tool-augmented actions to navigate this memory structure, allowing the model to reason effectively with only 2% of the full context. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks by efficiently managing long-range dependencies.

papers.title: Sparse Subspace-to-Expert Sharing for Task-Agnostic Continual Learning

SETA adaptively decomposes model parameters into task-specific experts and shared experts, isolating new knowledge while protecting common features. This separation, enforced by adaptive anchoring and routing-aware regularization, resolves the plasticity-stability dilemma without uniform parameter updates.
papers.figure_caption: Overview of the SETA Framework Architecture. (a–b) Sparse Subspace Selection: High-utility parameter blocks are identified from the pre-trained LLM using gradient magnitude to form the expert design. (c) Split-on-Share (SoS) Evolution: The SoS filter partitions parameters into plastic Shared ( E s E_{\( \text{s} \)} ) and frozen Unique ( E u E_{\( \text{u} \)} ) experts to resolve parameter collisions and retain knowledge. (d) Gating Evaluation: The gating network expands using logit invariance to strictly preserve decision boundaries during expert splitting. (e) Task-Agnostic Inference: A router network dynamically weights all experts via softmax for input tokens, enabling automatic task processing without task identifications.

papers.abstract: This paper introduces a controlled framework using real-world cultural questions to disentangle general language proficiency from localized cultural knowledge access in LLMs. By crossing question type (agnostic vs. specific) with query language (English vs. local) and employing Item Response Theory, the authors isolate the true impact of language choice. The core contribution is demonstrating a consistent English advantage even for culture-specific knowledge, suggesting local language access remains suboptimal.
papers.figure_caption: For cultural questions, local knowledge may be masked by weaker local-language proficiency.

papers.title: Watch, Remember, Reason: Human-View Video Understanding with MLLMs
papers.abstract: This paper proposes a unified framework for analyzing human-view video understanding using MLLMs, structured around three core abilities: **watching, remembering, and reasoning**. The contribution lies in providing a structured formulation to characterize how these models acquire evidence, maintain context over long videos, and perform grounded inference, moving beyond isolated benchmark testing. This approach helps systematically identify challenges in perception, memory management, and reasoning for video MLLMs.
papers.figure_caption: Overview of our survey. Left: the survey pipeline. Right: our Watch–Remember–Reason taxonomy for MLLM-based video understanding. Watch (Sec. 3.1 ) covers fine-grained grounding, captioning, audio-visual perception, and efficient processing. Remember (Sec. 3.2 ) includes offline and streaming memory. Reason (Sec. 3.3 ) covers text-only reasoning and thinking with videos, with both agentic and non-agent approaches. Representative methods are listed under each leaf.

papers.abstract: The paper introduces the **Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence (TBER)**, a framework explaining how new levels of representation arise in machine learning. TBER posits that representational innovation is driven not just by data or compute, but fundamentally by **explanatory insufficiency**, where existing representations can describe observations but fail to make their underlying organization intelligible. This explanatory gap acts as a positive signal, compelling the system to learn a new, more adequate representation.

hacker_news.title: The OnlyFans Economy of American AI
hacker_news.summary: The story likely explores the economic impact and dynamics of American AI, possibly drawing a provocative comparison to the content-driven, subscription-based "OnlyFans economy."

hacker_news.title: If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes, Then So Does Age of Empires II
hacker_news.summary: Researchers suggest that if Large Language Models exhibit human-like attributes, then classic strategy games like Age of Empires II should also be considered to possess similar characteristics.

github_trending.description: AI agent skill that researches any topic across Reddit, X, YouTube, HN, Polymarket, and the web - then synthesizes a grounded summary
github_trending.summary: This AI agent skill researches diverse online sources like Reddit, X, YouTube, and the web to synthesize grounded summaries on any given topic.

github_trending.description: A vector index built on TurboQuant, written in Rust with Python bindings
github_trending.summary: TurboVec is a Rust-based vector index leveraging TurboQuant, offering high-performance vector similarity search capabilities accessible via Python bindings.

github_trending.description: Agent Skills for Google products and technologies
github_trending.summary: Google's Skills repository provides agents with the necessary capabilities to interact with and leverage Google products and technologies.

github_trending.description: Desktop app to manage markdown knowledge bases
github_trending.summary: Tolaria is a desktop application designed to manage markdown knowledge bases, offering features for organizing and interacting with your notes.

github_trending.description: Give your AI agent eyes to see the entire internet. Read & search Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, GitHub, Bilibili, XiaoHongShu — one CLI, zero API fees.
github_trending.summary: Agent-Reach provides a unified command-line interface for AI agents to read and search content across major platforms like Twitter, Reddit, and YouTube without incurring API fees.

github_trending.description: Agentic AI Infrastructure for magnifying HUMAN capabilities.
github_trending.summary: This repository provides agentic AI infrastructure designed to significantly amplify human capabilities through automated, intelligent systems.

github_trending.description: AI-powered job search system built on Claude Code. 14 skill modes, Go dashboard, PDF generation, batch processing.
github_trending.summary: Career-Ops is an AI-powered job search system utilizing Claude Code, featuring 14 skill modes, a Go dashboard, PDF generation, and batch processing capabilities.

github_trending.description: PM Skills Marketplace: 100+ agentic skills, commands, and plugins — from discovery to strategy, execution, launch, and growth.
github_trending.summary: The **pm-skills** repository offers a marketplace of over 100 agentic skills, commands, and plugins designed to support the entire product management lifecycle, from initial discovery through strategy, execution, launch, and growth.

The OpenAI Plugins repository provides the necessary specifications and tools for developers to create and integrate third-party services with ChatGPT, enabling expanded functionality through external APIs.
